lists.title: Hindi A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: First Hindi in communicative order: SOV word order, the three 'you's, होना, noun gender, plurals, postpositions, possession, the simple present, negation, questions, commands, and having things.

lists.title: Hindi A2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Building sentences: the oblique case, past था, the present continuous, the simple past, the ergative ने, the future, wanting/needing, सकना, comparisons, and the subjunctive.

lists.title: Hindi B1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Connected speech: compound verbs, the perfect tenses, three obligations, relative–correlatives, conditionals, the passive, manner adverbs, and experiencer subjects.

lists.title: Hindi B2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Nuance and control: causatives, the presumptive, aspect light-verbs, reported speech, participial phrases, and Hindi's irregular numbers.

lists.title: Hindi C1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Register and fluency: Sanskritic vs Perso-Arabic vocabulary, honorific concord, body-and-mind idioms, and discourse particles.

lists.title: Hindi C2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Mastery: ergative edge cases and split ergativity, and literary/poetic syntax with Urdu izāfat.

points.title: Word order: subject–object–verb
points.function: Build your first sentences — the verb goes LAST
points.explanation: Hindi sentences put the verb at the end: मैं सेब खाता हूँ (I apple eat-am) = 'I eat an apple.' Everything else — time, place, objects — comes before the verb, usually in the order subject, time, place, object, verb. There is no word for 'a' or 'the': किताब can mean 'a book' or 'the book' depending on context.
points.culture_note: Because the verb carries person and gender endings, Hindi often drops pronouns once the subject is clear — context does the work.

points.title: Pronouns and politeness: तू, तुम, आप
points.function: Choose the right 'you' for the person in front of you
points.explanation: Hindi has three words for 'you': तू (intimate — small children, God, very close friends; easily rude), तुम (informal — friends, younger people; takes plural-style verb forms), and आप (respectful — elders, strangers, teachers; takes plural verb forms). The other pronouns: मैं (I), हम (we), यह/ये (this/these, he/she nearby), वह/वे (that/those, he/she far). Hindi has no separate 'he' vs 'she' — यह and वह cover both; the VERB ending shows gender.
points.culture_note: When in doubt, use आप. Using तू with a stranger is genuinely offensive, and many families use आप even with each other.

points.title: The verb 'to be': हूँ, है, हो, हैं
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation: होना (to be) in the present: मैं हूँ (I am), यह/वह है (he/she/it is), तुम हो (you are, informal), हम/आप/ये/वे हैं (we/you-formal/they are). Unlike Russian or Arabic, Hindi NEVER drops the copula: 'I student' is wrong; you must say मैं छात्र हूँ. The nasal marks matter: हूँ and हैं are nasalized, है is not.

points.title: Noun gender: masculine and feminine
points.function: Every noun has a gender — and the whole sentence agrees with it
points.explanation: Every Hindi noun is masculine or feminine. Rules of thumb: nouns ending in -ा are usually masculine (लड़का boy, कमरा room), nouns ending in -ी are usually feminine (लड़की girl, कुर्सी chair). But many nouns end in neither (घर house — masc., किताब book — fem., मेज़ table — fem.) and must be memorized with their gender. Gender controls verb endings and adjective endings, so it is not decoration — खाता हूँ (male speaker) vs खाती हूँ (female speaker).
points.culture_note: Loanwords get genders too: ट्रेन (train) is feminine, फ़ोन masculine. Native speakers disagree about some recent loans.

references.title: Hindi: An Essential Grammar (McGregor, OUP outline)

drills.sentence: मैं चाय पीत{{answer}} हूँ। (a woman speaking)
drills.hint: the verb agrees with the speaker's gender

drills.sentence: मैं खाना बनात{{answer}} हूँ। (a man speaking)

points.title: Plurals: लड़के, लड़कियाँ, किताबें
points.function: Talk about more than one
points.explanation: Masculine -ा nouns change to -े: लड़का → लड़के (boys), कमरा → कमरे. Masculine nouns NOT ending in -ा stay the same: घर → घर (houses). Feminine -ी nouns take -ियाँ: लड़की → लड़कियाँ (girls). Other feminine nouns take -ें: किताब → किताबें (books), मेज़ → मेज़ें. Adjectives and verbs agree: अच्छे लड़के (good boys), अच्छी लड़कियाँ (good girls).

drills.translation: There are four books on the table.
There are ten rooms in this building.

drills.hint: unmarked masculine — plural looks the same

points.title: Postpositions: में, पर, से, को
points.function: Say in, on, from, and to — AFTER the noun
points.explanation: Where English has prepositions, Hindi has POSTpositions — they follow the noun: घर में (in the house), मेज़ पर (on the table), दिल्ली से (from Delhi), राम को (to Ram). The big four: में (in), पर (on/at), से (from/with/by), को (to — marks the receiver, and specific objects). The noun before a postposition changes to its oblique form — covered in its own lesson soon.

drills.translation: The book is on the table.

points.title: Possession: का, के, की
points.function: Say whose it is — and agree with the thing owned
points.explanation: 'X's Y' is X का/के/की Y. The form agrees with the thing OWNED (Y), not the owner: राम का घर (Ram's house — masc. sing.), राम के घर (Ram's houses — masc. pl., and also before postpositions), राम की किताब (Ram's book — fem.). Personal pronouns have their own forms: मेरा/मेरे/मेरी (my), हमारा (our), तुम्हारा (your), आपका (your — respectful), उसका (his/her), उनका (their).

drills.translation: Ram has two brothers. (Ram's two brothers)

points.title: Simple present: करता हूँ, करती है, करते हैं
points.function: Talk about habits and general truths
points.explanation: The habitual present = verb stem + ता/ती/ते + the matching form of होना: मैं खाता हूँ (I eat — male), मैं खाती हूँ (I eat — female), वह खाता है (he eats), वह खाती है (she eats), हम खाते हैं (we eat), वे खाती हैं (they eat — all female). The participle agrees in GENDER and NUMBER with the subject; होना agrees in person. Use it for routines (मैं रोज़ चलता हूँ — I walk every day) and facts.

drills.sentence: मैं हर सुबह दौड़{{answer}} हूँ। (a man speaking)

points.title: Negation: नहीं and मत
points.function: Say no, not, and don't
points.explanation: नहीं goes right before the verb: मैं मांस नहीं खाता (I don't eat meat). In the habitual present, the final होना is usually dropped under negation: वह नहीं आता (he doesn't come — no है needed). For negative commands use मत: मत जाओ (don't go). ना is a softer 'don't' used with polite requests and in casual speech.

points.title: Questions: क्या and the question words
points.function: Ask yes/no questions and information questions
points.explanation: Sentence-initial क्या turns a statement into a yes/no question without changing word order: क्या आप चाय पीएँगे? (Will you have tea?). In speech, rising intonation alone also works. The question words sit where the answer would sit (usually just before the verb): क्या (what), कौन (who), कहाँ (where), कब (when), क्यों (why), कैसा/कैसे (how), कितना (how much), कौन-सा (which).

points.title: Commands and requests: करो, कीजिए
points.function: Tell, ask, and politely request
points.explanation: Each 'you' has its own command form. तुम: stem + ओ — बैठो (sit), आओ (come), करो (do). आप: stem + इए — बैठिए (please sit), कीजिए (please do), दीजिए (please give); irregulars: करना → कीजिए, देना → दीजिए, लेना → लीजिए, पीना → पीजिए. Adding ज़रा (just/kindly) or कृपया (please, formal) softens further. The bare stem is the तू command (बैठ!) — too blunt for learners to need.

points.title: Having things: के पास, का, को
points.function: Hindi has no verb 'to have' — here is what it uses instead
points.explanation: For possessions you can pick up: X के पास Y है — मेरे पास एक गाड़ी है (I have a car; literally 'near me a car is'). For relatives and body parts: का/के/की — राम के दो भाई हैं (Ram has two brothers). For states, feelings, and illnesses: को — मुझे बुख़ार है (I have a fever; 'to-me fever is'). Choosing the right one is the whole lesson: possessions → के पास, people → के, states → को.

drills.translation: He has a lot of money.

drills.hint: relatives use the possessive — sister is feminine
an object you can hand over

points.title: The oblique case: कमरे में, लड़के को
points.function: Nouns change shape before postpositions
points.explanation: Any noun directly followed by a postposition switches to its OBLIQUE form. Masculine -ा nouns: -ा → -े (कमरा → कमरे में, in the room; लड़का → लड़के को, to the boy). Their plurals: -े → -ों (लड़कों को, to the boys). Feminine nouns don't change in the singular (लड़की को) but take -ों in the plural (लड़कियों को). Unmarked nouns: singular unchanged, plural -ों (घरों में). Adjectives and possessives shift too: मेरा → मेरे (मेरे घर में), बड़ा → बड़े (बड़े कमरे में).

drills.translation: We live in a big house.
drills.hint: house — unmarked masc., unchanged in singular

drills.translation: The book is in the room.
The teacher talks with the children.
There is no one in my house.

points.title: Past 'to be': था, थी, थे, थीं
points.function: Say how things were
points.explanation: The past of होना agrees in gender and number: था (masc. sing.), थी (fem. sing.), थे (masc. pl. / आप), थीं (fem. pl.): मैं वहाँ था (I was there — male), वह ख़ुश थी (she was happy), हम दोस्त थे (we were friends), वे अध्यापिकाएँ थीं (they were teachers — female). Combine with the habitual participle for 'used to': मैं रोज़ दौड़ता था (I used to run every day).

drills.sentence: कल मैं घर पर {{answer}}। (a man speaking)

drills.translation: My grandmother was a teacher. (respect plural)
drills.hint: respected elders take the plural — feminine

points.title: Present continuous: रहा है, रही है, रहे हैं
points.function: Say what is happening right now
points.explanation: Stem + रहा/रही/रहे + होना: मैं खा रहा हूँ (I am eating — male), वह सो रही है (she is sleeping), हम पढ़ रहे हैं (we are studying). रहा agrees with the subject's gender and number; the stem never changes. Contrast with the habitual: मैं चाय पीता हूँ (I drink tea — in general) vs मैं चाय पी रहा हूँ (I am drinking tea — right now).

drills.sentence: मैं खाना बना {{answer}} हूँ। (a man speaking)
मेरी माँ फ़ोन पर बात कर {{answer}} हैं।
drills.translation: My mother is talking on the phone.

points.title: Simple past: गया, आई, पहुँचे
points.function: Say what happened — intransitive verbs first
points.explanation: For verbs without an object (go, come, arrive, sleep), the perfective is the stem + -ा/-ई/-ए agreeing with the subject: वह गया (he went), वह गई (she went), हम आए (we came), वे पहुँचीं (they arrived — female). Irregulars to memorize: जाना → गया, करना → किया, देना → दिया, लेना → लिया, पीना → पिया, होना → हुआ. Transitive verbs work differently (the ने lesson is next — this one sets it up).

drills.translation: The guests came in the evening.

drills.hint: happened — बारिश is feminine; होना is irregular

points.title: The ergative ने: मैंने किताब पढ़ी
points.function: Hindi's most famous trap — the verb agrees with the OBJECT
points.explanation: With a TRANSITIVE verb in the perfective (completed) past, the subject takes ने and the verb agrees with the OBJECT, not the subject: मैंने किताब पढ़ी (I read the book — पढ़ी is feminine because किताब is feminine, regardless of who reads). राम ने आम खाया (Ram ate a mango — खाया masc. for आम). Pronouns fuse: मैं+ने → मैंने, उस+ने → उसने, किस+ने → किसने; तुम → तुमने, आप → आपने. If the object carries को, the verb goes to neutral masculine singular: मैंने सीता को देखा.
points.culture_note: This split-ergative pattern is shared with Punjabi, Gujarati and Marathi — mastering it in Hindi buys you three more languages.

drills.hint: ate — agrees with the mango, not Sita

points.title: Future: जाऊँगा, करेगी, मिलेंगे
points.function: Talk about tomorrow
points.explanation: Future endings attach straight to the stem and agree in person AND gender: मैं जाऊँगा/जाऊँगी (I will go — m/f), तू जाएगा/जाएगी, वह करेगा/करेगी (he/she will do), तुम जाओगे/जाओगी, हम/वे जाएँगे/जाएँगी, आप जाएँगे. The pattern is the subjunctive stem + गा/गी/गे. Common irregular stems: होना → होगा (will be), देना → देगा, लेना → लेगा.

drills.translation: I will go to Mumbai tomorrow. (male)
We will meet in the evening.
They will go to India next year.

points.title: Wanting and needing: चाहिए and चाहना
points.function: Ask for things two different ways
points.explanation: मुझे X चाहिए = 'I need/want X' (literally 'to-me X is-wanted') — the wanter takes को, and चाहिए never changes for tense in this basic use: मुझे पानी चाहिए (I want water), उसे मदद चाहिए (he needs help). With a verb: मुझे जाना चाहिए (I should go — infinitive + चाहिए gives advice). The full verb चाहना means to want/love: मैं सीखना चाहता हूँ (I want to learn), वह डॉक्टर बनना चाहती है (she wants to become a doctor).

drills.translation: I want a cup of tea.

drills.sentence: मैं हिंदी सीखना {{answer}} हूँ। (a man speaking)

drills.translation: We want to go to India.

points.title: Can and could: सकना
points.function: Say what you are able to do
points.explanation: सकना never stands alone — it follows a bare verb stem: मैं कर सकता हूँ (I can do), वह आ सकती है (she can come), हम जीत सकते हैं (we can win). Past: वह नहीं आ सका (he couldn't come). Future: मैं आ सकूँगा (I'll be able to come). Its cousin पाना suggests managing despite difficulty: मैं सो नहीं पाया (I couldn't manage to sleep).

drills.sentence: मैं हिंदी बोल {{answer}} हूँ। (a man speaking)
मैं कल नहीं आ {{answer}}। (a man speaking)
बिना चश्मे के वह पढ़ नहीं {{answer}}।

points.title: Comparisons: से बड़ा, सबसे अच्छा
points.function: Compare things without 'more' or '-er'
points.explanation: Hindi compares with से: X, Y से बड़ा है = 'X is bigger than Y' (literally 'X, from Y, is big') — the adjective itself never changes: दिल्ली मुंबई से बड़ी है? No '-er', no 'more'. The superlative is सबसे (than all): सबसे अच्छा (the best), सबसे लंबी सड़क (the longest road). ज़्यादा (more) and कम (less) appear when there is no adjective: मुझे चाय से ज़्यादा कॉफ़ी पसंद है.

drills.translation: An elephant is bigger than a horse.

drills.sentence: यह किताब उस किताब {{answer}} अच्छी है।
drills.translation: This book is better than that book.

drills.sentence: गंगा भारत की {{answer}} लंबी नदी है।
drills.translation: The Ganga is the longest river in India.

drills.sentence: मुझे पानी चाय से {{answer}} पसंद है।
drills.translation: I like water more than tea.

points.title: Let's and may I: the subjunctive
points.function: Suggest, offer, and ask permission
points.explanation: The subjunctive is the future minus गा/गी/गे: चलें (let's go), बैठें (let's sit), मैं आऊँ? (may I come?), क्या मैं यह लूँ? (may I take this?). It appears in suggestions (चाय पिएँ? — shall we have tea?), permission requests, and after क़ि/ताकि, चाहना: मैं चाहता हूँ कि तुम आओ (I want you to come — literally 'I want that you come').

drills.hint: may I come — first person subjunctive

drills.sentence: मैं चाहता हूँ कि तुम रोज़ {{answer}}।
drills.translation: I want you to study every day.

points.title: Compound verbs: ले जाना, खा लेना
points.function: Add nuance — completion, suddenness, direction — with a light second verb
points.explanation: A main verb stem + a 'vector' verb adds aspect and colour. लेना (toward self / for one's own benefit): खा लेना (eat up), पढ़ लेना (read through). देना (away / for another): दे देना (give away), बता देना (tell). जाना (completion / change of state): सो जाना (fall asleep), मर जाना (die), आ जाना (arrive/turn up). डालना (do decisively/violently), बैठना (do something regrettable). The vector loses its literal meaning; the first stem stays bare.

drills.translation: I ate up all the food.
The child fell asleep at once.
drills.hint: change of state — past of जाना

points.title: The perfect tenses: चुका है, गया था
points.function: Say what has happened and what had happened
points.explanation: Present perfect = perfective participle + present होना: वह आया है (he has come), मैंने खाया है (I have eaten). Past perfect = perfective participle + था: वह आया था (he had come). A cleaner 'already done' is stem + चुकना: मैं खा चुका हूँ (I have already eaten), फ़िल्म शुरू हो चुकी है (the film has already begun). चुकना stresses completion and can't take an object with ने.

drills.translation: I have seen this film before.

drills.sentence: जब मैं पहुँचा, ट्रेन जा {{answer}} थी।
drills.translation: When I arrived, the train had already left.

points.title: Obligation: चाहिए, है, पड़ना
points.function: Three strengths of 'must' and 'have to'
points.explanation: Rising force: (1) infinitive + चाहिए = should/ought — मुझे जाना चाहिए (I should go). (2) infinitive + होना = am to / supposed to — मुझे कल जाना है (I have to go tomorrow, it's arranged). (3) infinitive + पड़ना = forced to by circumstance — मुझे जाना पड़ा (I had to go, no choice). The subject takes को (मुझे, उसे…), and the infinitive agrees with the object: मुझे यह किताब पढ़नी है (पढ़नी fem. for किताब).

drills.translation: You should go to the doctor.
I have to go to Delhi tomorrow.
We had to stop in the rain.
I have to take this medicine.
He has to get up early every day.

points.title: Relative–correlative: जो… वह, जब… तब
points.function: Join clauses the Hindi way — with matching pairs
points.explanation: Hindi links clauses with paired words rather than a single 'which/when'. जो… वह (the one who / that which… that): जो लड़का वहाँ खड़ा है, वह मेरा भाई है (the boy who is standing there, he is my brother). Other pairs: जब… तब (when… then), जहाँ… वहाँ (where… there), जैसा… वैसा (as… so), जितना… उतना (as much as… that much), जितने… उतने. The जो-clause usually comes FIRST; the correlative (वह/तब/वहाँ) picks it up in the main clause.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} मेहनत करता है, वह सफल होता है।
drills.translation: The one who works hard succeeds.

drills.sentence: जो किताब मेज़ पर है, {{answer}} मेरी है।
drills.translation: The book that is on the table is mine.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} मैं छोटा था, तब गाँव में रहता था।
drills.translation: When I was little, I lived in a village.

drills.sentence: जब बारिश होगी, {{answer}} हम घर रहेंगे।
drills.translation: When it rains, then we'll stay home.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} तुम रहते हो, वहाँ मैं भी रहना चाहता हूँ।
drills.translation: Where you live, I want to live there too.
The more you study, the more you'll learn.

points.title: Conditionals: अगर… तो
points.function: Talk about ifs — real and imagined
points.explanation: Real/open condition: अगर (or यदि) … तो with future or present: अगर बारिश होगी तो हम नहीं जाएँगे (if it rains, we won't go). Unreal/contrary-to-fact: both clauses use the imperfective participle + होता: अगर मैं अमीर होता तो घर ख़रीदता (if I were rich, I would buy a house — but I'm not). तो ('then') opens the main clause and is often kept even when अगर is dropped.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} तुम पढ़ोगे तो पास हो जाओगे।
अगर वह आएगा {{answer}} मैं ख़ुश हूँगा।
drills.translation: If he comes, then I'll be happy.

drills.sentence: अगर मैं अमीर {{answer}} तो बड़ा घर ख़रीदता।
drills.translation: If I were rich, I would buy a big house.

drills.sentence: अगर तुमने बताया {{answer}}, तो मैं मदद करता।
drills.translation: If you had told me, I would have helped.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} वह सच बोलती तो सब ठीक होता।
drills.translation: If she told the truth, everything would be fine.

points.title: The passive: खाया जाता है
points.function: Say something is done without naming the doer
points.explanation: Passive = perfective participle + जाना (conjugated): यहाँ हिंदी बोली जाती है (Hindi is spoken here), खाना बनाया जाता है (food is made), दरवाज़ा खोला गया (the door was opened). The participle agrees with the grammatical subject (बोली fem. for हिंदी). The agent, if named, takes के द्वारा or से. Passive of ability/incapacity: मुझसे यह नहीं खाया जाता (I can't (bring myself to) eat this).

drills.sentence: भारत में कई भाषाएँ {{answer}} जाती हैं।
drills.translation: Many languages are spoken in India.
drills.hint: passive participle, fem. pl. for भाषाएँ

drills.sentence: यह मिठाई दूध से {{answer}} जाती है।
drills.translation: This sweet is made from milk.
The letter will be sent tomorrow.
I just can't do this work.

drills.sentence: यह किताब बच्चों के लिए {{answer}} गई है।
drills.translation: This book was written for children.

points.title: Adverbs and manner: से, कर के, तरह
points.function: Say how, and chain actions together
points.explanation: Turn an adjective into an adverb with से: ध्यान से (attentively), आराम से (comfortably), जल्दी से (quickly). The conjunctive participle stem + कर (or के) means 'having done…, then…', chaining actions with one shared subject: खाना खाकर मैं सोया (having eaten, I slept), नहाकर वह गया (he bathed and left). की तरह = 'like' (शेर की तरह — like a lion); के बारे में = 'about'.

drills.translation: Having eaten, we went for a walk.
He is brave like a lion.
After bathing, he went to the office.

drills.sentence: मुझे इस विषय के {{answer}} में बताइए।

points.title: Experiencer subjects: मुझे लगता है, पसंद है
points.function: Feelings, opinions, and liking — the subject takes को
points.explanation: Many feeling/perception verbs make the experiencer take को (मुझे, तुम्हें, उसे…) and agree with the THING felt: मुझे यह पसंद है (I like this — 'to-me this is pleasing'), मुझे भूख लगी है (I'm hungry), मुझे लगता है कि… (I think that…), मुझे मालूम है (I know), उसे ग़ुस्सा आया (he got angry), मुझे हिंदी आती है (I know Hindi — 'Hindi comes to me'). What you like/know is the grammatical subject, so it drives the verb's gender.

drills.sentence: मुझे {{answer}} है कि वह सही है।
drills.translation: I think that he is right.
Do you know how to swim?

points.title: Causatives: करना → कराना, बनना → बनाना
points.function: Say you got something done — by someone else
points.explanation: Hindi derives causatives systematically. Direct causative (I make X happen): often -ा-: बनना (to be made) → बनाना (to make), पढ़ना (to read) → पढ़ाना (to teach = cause to read), उठना (to rise) → उठाना (to lift). Indirect/second causative (I have it done via an agent): -वा-: बनवाना (to have made), पढ़वाना (to have taught), कटवाना (to get cut). The agent takes से: मैंने दर्ज़ी से कमीज़ सिलवाई (I had the tailor sew a shirt).

drills.translation: The mother feeds the child. (makes eat)
The teachers teach the children Hindi.

drills.hint: indirect causative of काटना — via a barber

drills.translation: He got me to do this work.
We had a new house built.

points.title: Presumptive: होगा, रहा होगा
points.function: Make educated guesses about now and the past
points.explanation: होगा/होगी/होंगे double as the presumptive ('probably is/will be'): वह घर पर होगा (he's probably home). Add participles for shaded guesses: वह सो रहा होगा (he must be sleeping — presumptive continuous), उसने खाया होगा (he must have eaten — presumptive perfect), वह रोज़ आता होगा (he probably comes daily — presumptive habitual). शायद (maybe) and ज़रूर (surely) tune the confidence.

drills.sentence: अभी दस बजे हैं, वह दफ़्तर में {{answer}}।
drills.translation: It's ten now; he must be at the office.

drills.sentence: वे थके होंगे, आराम कर {{answer}} होंगे।
drills.translation: They're tired; they must be resting.

drills.sentence: उसने अब तक खाना खा {{answer}} होगा।
drills.translation: He must have eaten by now.

drills.sentence: तुम्हारी बहन अब बड़ी हो {{answer}} होगी।
drills.translation: Your sister must have grown up by now.
He probably comes here every day.

points.title: Aspect verbs: लगना, रहना, चुकना, जाना
points.function: Say something began, kept on, or gradually became
points.explanation: Beyond simple tenses, light verbs mark aspect. Stem-oblique + लगना = to start/begin: वह रोने लगा (he began to cry), बारिश होने लगी. Stem + रहना (with the -ता participle it's habitual continuity; as a vector it's persistence): पढ़ता रहा (kept studying), वह बैठा रहा (he remained seated). Present/past + जाना for gradual change: बड़ा होता जाता है (keeps getting bigger). किए जाना = keep on doing: वह बोले जा रहा था (he kept on talking).

drills.translation: The child suddenly began to cry.
The rain began to fall harder.
He kept studying till late at night.
People kept coming; the hall filled up.

drills.sentence: वह बिना रुके बोले {{answer}} रहा था।
drills.translation: He kept on talking without stopping.
drills.hint: किए जाना pattern — stem of जाना

points.title: Reported speech and कि-clauses
points.function: Report what people said and thought
points.explanation: Hindi reports speech with कि but does NOT shift tense or pronoun the way English does — it quotes almost directly: उसने कहा कि मैं आऊँगा (he said that 'I will come', i.e. he said he'd come — मैं stays first person!). This trips up English speakers: राम ने कहा कि वह थका है (Ram said that he is tired). कि also follows verbs of thinking/knowing/fearing: मुझे डर है कि… (I'm afraid that…), मुझे उम्मीद है कि… (I hope that…).

drills.translation: He said that he would come tomorrow.

drills.sentence: राम ने कहा कि {{answer}} भूखा हूँ।
drills.translation: Ram said that he was hungry. (lit. 'I am hungry')
drills.hint: Hindi keeps the original 'I' — no pronoun shift

drills.sentence: मुझे {{answer}} है कि तुम पास हो जाओगे।
drills.translation: I hope that you will pass.

drills.sentence: उसने पूछा कि मैं {{answer}} जा रहा हूँ।
drills.translation: He asked where I was going.

drills.sentence: मुझे {{answer}} है कि वह नहीं आएगा।
drills.translation: I'm afraid that he won't come.

drills.sentence: शिक्षक ने बताया {{answer}} परीक्षा सोमवार को है।
drills.translation: The teacher told (us) that the exam is on Monday.

points.title: Participial phrases: आते हुए, बैठे-बैठे
points.function: Add rich 'while/-ing' description
points.explanation: The imperfective participle + हुए means 'while ­-ing': घर आते हुए मैंने उसे देखा (while coming home, I saw him), मुस्कुराते हुए (smilingly). The perfective participle + हुए describes a resulting state: कुर्सी पर बैठा हुआ आदमी (a man seated on a chair), थका हुआ (tired). Doubling a participle stresses duration: बैठे-बैठे थक गया (got tired just from sitting), सोचते-सोचते (while pondering on and on). All agree in gender/number with what they modify.

drills.translation: While coming home, I saw the market.

drills.sentence: कुर्सी पर {{answer}} हुआ आदमी मेरा चाचा है।
drills.translation: The man seated on the chair is my uncle.

drills.sentence: मेज़ पर {{answer}} हुई किताबें मेरी हैं।
drills.translation: The books placed on the table are mine.
drills.hint: resulting state — fem. pl. for किताबें

drills.translation: Just sitting and sitting, I got bored.

points.title: Numbers, ordinals, and fractions
points.function: Handle Hindi's notoriously irregular counting
points.explanation: Hindi cardinals are irregular through 100 and must be learned in blocks (इक्कीस 21, उनतीस 29, इकतालीस 41…) — each ten's transition is unpredictable. Ordinals: पहला (1st), दूसरा (2nd), तीसरा (3rd), चौथा (4th), then number + वाँ (पाँचवाँ 5th, दसवाँ 10th). Fractions have special words: आधा (½), पौना (¾ / 'quarter to'), सवा (1¼), डेढ़ (1½), ढाई (2½). Telling time and money leans on these constantly: सवा तीन बजे (3:15), डेढ़ सौ (150).

drills.translation: He came second in the class.
I need half a kilo of sugar.
Give me one hundred fifty rupees. (150)
This is the building's fifth floor.

points.title: Register: तत्सम Sanskrit vs Perso-Arabic vocabulary
points.function: Read the room — शुद्ध हिंदी, everyday Hindustani, or Urdu-leaning speech
points.explanation: Hindi–Urdu share a grammar but draw vocabulary from two wells. Sanskritic (तत्सम) words dominate formal, literary, and governmental Hindi: धन्यवाद (thanks), प्रश्न (question), विद्यालय (school), समाचार (news). Perso-Arabic words dominate everyday speech, poetry, and Urdu: शुक्रिया (thanks), सवाल (question), स्कूल, ख़बर. A fluent speaker code-switches by audience: a news anchor says प्रश्न, a friend says सवाल. Choosing the wrong register sounds stilted or, conversely, too colloquial for the setting.
points.culture_note: The nuqta (ख़, ग़, ज़, फ़, क़) marks Perso-Arabic sounds; dropping it (खबर for ख़बर) is common in casual writing but changes the 'correct' pronunciation.

drills.sentence: औपचारिक भाषण में 'धन्यवाद' का रोज़मर्रा पर्याय {{answer}} है।
समाचार में वे 'सवाल' के बजाय {{answer}} कहते हैं।
drills.translation: In the news they say 'praśn' instead of 'savāl'.

drills.sentence: उर्दू-रंग वाली हिंदी में 'समाचार' को {{answer}} कहेंगे।
drills.translation: In Urdu-leaning Hindi, 'samāchār' would be called 'khabar'.

drills.sentence: साहित्यिक हिंदी 'शुक्रिया' के लिए {{answer}} पसंद करती है।

points.title: Honorific concord and pronoun avoidance
points.function: Show respect through grammar, not just word choice
points.explanation: Respect is grammaticalized. A single respected person takes PLURAL agreement throughout: पिताजी आए हैं (Father has come — plural आए/हैं for one person), गुरुजी क्या कहते हैं? Third-person respect uses वे/उन्होंने even for one person. Polished speech often AVOIDS direct pronouns for elders, using titles or teknonyms (आपकी तबियत कैसी है? rather than तुम). Over-respecting a peer can sound sarcastic; under-respecting an elder is a real offense. जी appended to names/words (जी हाँ, राम जी) and standalone जी signal deference.

drills.translation: Father has just come home. (honorific plural)
drills.hint: plural participle for one respected person

drills.translation: Grandmother has gone to the market.
drills.hint: honorific — fem. plural गईं/गई for one elder

drills.sentence: गुरुजी {{answer}} कहते हैं, वह सही है।
drills.translation: Whatever the teacher says is right.
drills.hint: relative pronoun; verb stays plural for respect

drills.sentence: आदरपूर्वक हाँ कहने के लिए '{{answer}} हाँ' कहते हैं।
drills.translation: To say yes respectfully, one says 'jī hā̃'.
It was they (respected) who made this decision.
drills.hint: ने-past with उन्होंने for one honored person

points.title: Idiomatic body-and-mind expressions
points.function: Speak like a native — feelings that live in the liver, not the heart
points.explanation: Fluent Hindi runs on fixed expressions that don't translate literally. मन (mind/heart) drives desire: मेरा मन करता है (I feel like), मन लगना (to feel settled/engaged). दिल for emotion: दिल टूटना (heart to break). सिर for burdens: सिर पर सवार होना (to be obsessed with). Verbs like पड़ना, आना, लगना build hundreds: याद आना (to remember), नींद आना (to feel sleepy), काम आना (to be of use), हाथ लगना (to obtain). These experiencer idioms take को and are non-negotiable for sounding natural.

drills.sentence: मेरा {{answer}} आज घूमने का कर रहा है।
drills.translation: I feel like going out today.

drills.sentence: मुझे अचानक अपनी माँ की {{answer}} आई।

drills.translation: This book was useful in the exam.

drills.sentence: उसकी बात सुनकर मेरा {{answer}} टूट गया।
drills.translation: Hearing his words, my heart broke.

drills.sentence: नए शहर में उसका {{answer}} नहीं लगा।
drills.translation: He didn't feel settled in the new city.

points.title: Discourse particles: तो, ही, भी, न, वाला
points.function: Master the little words that carry all the attitude
points.explanation: Untranslatable particles do heavy lifting. तो marks contrast/topic/emphasis (मैं तो जा रहा हूँ — as for me, I'm going). ही = 'only/exactly/the very' (आज ही — this very day, वही — that exact one). भी = 'also/even' (मैं भी, एक भी नहीं — not even one). न softens or seeks agreement (चलो न — come on, आप आएँगे न? — you'll come, right?). वाला forms 'the one that/who' and imminence (जाने वाला — about to go, दूधवाला — the milkman, लाल वाली — the red one). Placement changes scope, so these reward careful ear-training.

drills.sentence: मैं {{answer}} यह काम आज ही करूँगा।
drills.translation: As for me, I'll do this very task today.
Finish this work this very day.
I will go with you too.
The train is about to arrive.

points.title: Ne-construction edge cases and split ergativity
points.function: Handle the ergative's genuine exceptions like a native
points.explanation: The ने rule has real exceptions that even heritage speakers slip on. Some transitive verbs NEVER take ने: लाना (to bring — मैं किताब लाया, not लाई with ने), भूलना (to forget), बोलना and मिलना in many uses. Conjunct verbs split: बात करना takes ने (मैंने बात की) but the light-verb choice matters. When both a को-marked object and ने appear, the verb defaults to masculine singular (मैंने लड़कियों को देखा — देखा, not देखीं). Compound verbs with intransitive vectors can suppress ने (वह किताब पढ़ गया). Mastery means knowing which verbs opt out.

drills.translation: I brought fruit from the market yesterday. (male)
drills.hint: लाना never takes ने — plain agreement with speaker

drills.translation: She placed her books on the table.
drills.hint: ने-past, verb agrees with किताबें (fem. pl.)
को-marked object → verb goes neutral masc. sing.

drills.sentence: वह अपना बचपन का घर {{answer}} गया।
drills.hint: भूलना avoids ने — stem before the जाना vector

drills.sentence: हमने इस मुद्दे पर लंबी बात {{answer}}।
drills.translation: We talked at length about this issue.
drills.hint: बात करना takes ने; की agrees with बात (fem.)

drills.translation: The mother (went and) scolded the child.

points.title: Literary and poetic syntax: inversion, ellipsis, Urdu izafat
points.function: Read ग़ज़ल, film lyrics, and high literature
points.explanation: Elevated registers bend the everyday rules. Word order inverts for emphasis or metre — the verb may precede its subject, objects front: सुना है लोगों से (I've heard from people…). The Persian izāfat (-e-) links noun to modifier in Urdu-heritage phrases: शमा-ए-महफ़िल (the gathering's flame), तर्ज़-ए-बयाँ (manner of expression). Postpositions and even the copula drop in verse (दिल है कि मानता नहीं). Compound Persian/Sanskrit tatpuruṣa formations pack whole phrases into single words. Comprehension here rests on recognizing when the standard grammar is being deliberately suspended.
points.culture_note: Bollywood lyrics are the mass-culture gateway to this register — a single film song can carry izāfat, inversion, and dropped copulas in one line.

drills.sentence: काव्य में अक्सर क्रिया कर्ता से {{answer}} आ जाती है।
drills.translation: In poetry the verb often comes before the subject.

drills.sentence: 'दिल है कि मानता नहीं' में कर्ता के बाद की {{answer}} छूट गई है।
drills.translation: In 'dil hai ki māntā nahī̃', the expected verb is elided.

drills.hint: the possessive the izāfat stands in for

drills.sentence: उच्च शैली में कभी-कभी परसर्ग {{answer}} लोप हो जाता है।
drills.translation: In high style, the postposition sometimes drops.

drills.sentence: फ़िल्मी गीत इस काव्य-शैली का लोकप्रिय {{answer}} हैं।
drills.translation: Film songs are a popular gateway to this poetic style.
